lessons.title: The FPGA Fabric: What's Actually on the Die
lessons.content:
Imagine a chip full of generic digital building blocks whose wiring you can rearrange *after* the chip is made — like reprogrammable electronic LEGO, or a breadboard you wire up by software instead of by hand. That is an **FPGA** (Field-Programmable Gate Array). "Field-programmable" means you configure it out in the world, not at the factory; you load a configuration file called a **bitstream**, and the chip *becomes* whatever digital circuit you described.

This is very different from a CPU. A CPU runs a list of instructions roughly one at a time. An FPGA instead builds **actual hardware** for your design, so many things happen truly at the same time (in parallel). That makes FPGAs great at doing lots of work at once.

So what is inside the chip? A repeating grid called the **fabric**, made of a few kinds of block:

- **Logic blocks** — the basic unit. Each one is a **lookup table (LUT)** plus a **flip-flop**. A LUT is a tiny memory that can act as *any* small logic gate (you fill in its answers). A flip-flop is a 1-bit memory that remembers a value between clock ticks. Together they can build any logic.
- **Block RAM** — small chunks of on-chip memory for storing data, like buffers or lists of numbers.
- **DSP blocks** — built-in fast multipliers, handy for math-heavy jobs.
- **Clock blocks (PLLs)** — make and steady the clock signals.
- **I/O blocks** — connect the fabric to the chip's pins, so it can talk to LEDs, buttons, and other parts.

**Recap:** an FPGA is reconfigurable hardware. A bitstream sets every block and wire in a fabric of logic blocks (LUT + flip-flop), block RAM, DSP, clocking, and I/O.

items.prompt: What does "field-programmable" mean in Field-Programmable Gate Array?
choices: You can configure the chip out in the world after it is manufactured, not just at the factory
It only works outdoors in an open field
Its program is permanently set during manufacturing and cannot change
It can only run one fixed program written by the chip vendor
items.explanation: "Field-programmable" means you, the user, configure the chip in the field (after manufacturing) by loading a configuration. That is exactly what lets one FPGA become many different circuits. A chip whose function is fixed at the factory would be the opposite of field-programmable.

items.prompt: What is a bitstream?
choices: The configuration file you load to tell the FPGA what circuit to become
A live stream of measurement data flowing out of the chip
The chip's power supply waveform
A list of CPU instructions the FPGA executes one by one
items.explanation: A bitstream is the configuration file. Loading it sets every block and wire inside the FPGA so the generic fabric becomes your specific design. It is not data you stream during use, and an FPGA does not execute instructions like a CPU — it builds hardware.

items.prompt: What is a LUT (lookup table) and what is it for?
choices: A tiny memory that can be filled in to act as any small logic gate
A large external memory chip that stores the whole program
A multiplier used for heavy math
A pin that connects the chip to a button
items.explanation: A LUT is a small memory that holds the answer for each combination of its inputs — so by filling in those answers you make it behave like AND, OR, XOR, or any small logic function. That flexibility is why FPGA logic is called "LUT-based." Large memory, multipliers, and pins are separate kinds of block.

items.prompt: A logic block in the fabric is made of which two parts?
choices: A lookup table (LUT) and a flip-flop
A multiplier and a memory bank
A clock generator and an I/O pin
Two CPUs working together
items.explanation: The basic logic block is a LUT (to compute any small logic function) plus a flip-flop (to remember one bit between clock ticks). Thousands of these, plus programmable wiring, form the fabric. Multipliers (DSP), memory (block RAM), clocks, and I/O are other, separate block types.

items.prompt: What does a flip-flop do inside a logic block?
choices: Remembers a single bit, holding its value between clock ticks
Multiplies two numbers very quickly
Connects the design to a physical pin
Stores thousands of data samples at once
items.explanation: A flip-flop is a 1-bit memory clocked by the clock signal: it captures and holds one bit until the next tick. That is what lets a circuit have state and step through time. Bulk storage is block RAM's job, multiplying is the DSP block's job, and pins are I/O blocks.

items.prompt: You need to store a buffer of about 16,000 data samples inside the FPGA. Which block is the right choice?
choices: Block RAM (on-chip memory)
A pile of individual flip-flops
DSP blocks (multipliers)
The I/O blocks
items.explanation: Block RAM is dedicated on-chip memory made for buffers and lists of values. Storing 16,000 samples in individual flip-flops would waste a huge part of the fabric. DSP blocks do math, not bulk storage, and I/O blocks just connect to pins.

items.prompt: What is a DSP block in an FPGA mainly used for?
choices: Doing fast multiplication (and add) for math-heavy work
Holding large buffers of data
Connecting to LEDs and buttons
Generating and cleaning up the clock
items.explanation: A DSP block is a built-in fast multiplier (usually with an adder too), so multiply-heavy math runs quickly without wasting general logic. Bulk storage is block RAM, pins are I/O, and clock generation is the clock (PLL) block.

items.prompt: What is the clearest difference between an FPGA and a CPU?
choices: An FPGA builds real parallel hardware so many things run at once, while a CPU runs instructions roughly one at a time
An FPGA always runs at a much higher clock speed than any CPU
An FPGA can only do memory storage, never logic
A CPU can be reconfigured into any circuit but an FPGA cannot
items.explanation: An FPGA spends chip area to lay down actual hardware that all runs in parallel; a CPU reuses one datapath to step through instructions in sequence. FPGAs often run at a *lower* clock speed than CPUs but win by doing many things at once. It is the FPGA, not the CPU, that is reconfigurable into different circuits.

items.prompt: Why is one FPGA able to become many completely different digital circuits?
choices: Its blocks and wiring are generic and are set by the bitstream you load
It physically rearranges its silicon atoms each time
It downloads a new factory mask every time it powers up
It contains a copy of every possible circuit already built in
items.explanation: The fabric is generic: LUTs can be filled to do any small logic, and the wires between blocks are switchable. The bitstream sets all of that, so loading a different bitstream gives a different circuit. The silicon itself never changes; only its configuration does.

items.prompt: Which everyday comparison best captures what an FPGA is?
choices: Reprogrammable electronic LEGO, or a breadboard you wire up by software
A sealed appliance that does exactly one job and can never change
A hard disk that only stores files
A battery that supplies power
items.explanation: An FPGA is a kit of generic building blocks you connect however you like by loading a configuration — like LEGO or a software-wired breadboard. A fixed single-purpose appliance is closer to an ASIC. Storage and power are unrelated functions.

items.prompt: A logic cell built from a LUT and a flip-flop.
choices: The LUT computes any small logic function of its inputs, and the flip-flop can optionally store that result for one clock tick
The LUT only adds numbers, and the flip-flop only connects to pins
The LUT is external memory, and the flip-flop is the clock source
The LUT and flip-flop both just store bulk data
items.explanation: In a logic cell the LUT does the logic (any small function of its inputs) and the flip-flop optionally registers the output so the value is held to the next clock tick. Together, many of these cells plus programmable wiring make up the fabric.

items.prompt: The repeating grid of blocks and programmable wires inside an FPGA is called the:
choices: Fabric
Bitstream
Bus
Datasheet
items.explanation: The fabric is the grid of logic blocks, block RAM, DSP, clocking, and I/O, tied together by switchable wiring. The bitstream is the file that configures the fabric; a bus is a shared set of wires for moving data; a datasheet is documentation.

lessons.title: The Implementation Flow: HDL to Bitstream
lessons.content:
You already met **HDL** (Hardware Description Language — text that describes a circuit) in the last sub-topic. But writing HDL is only the start. To get your design running on a real FPGA, the tools take it through a pipeline of steps, a bit like how source code becomes a running program — except the end result is hardware, not software.

Here is the whole journey, one step at a time:

1. **Write the HDL.** Describe the circuit you want in Verilog or VHDL.
2. **Simulate.** Before touching real hardware, run your design in a simulator with a **testbench** (test code that feeds in inputs and checks the outputs). Catching a bug here takes seconds; catching it on a board can take hours.
3. **Synthesize.** The tool translates your HDL into the FPGA's actual building blocks — LUTs, flip-flops, block RAM, DSP. The result is a list of blocks and connections called a **netlist**.
4. **Place and route.** "Place" decides which physical spot on the chip each block goes; "route" connects them through the chip's wiring. This is the slow, hard step, because there are millions of choices.
5. **Generate the bitstream.** Pack the finished design into the binary configuration file.
6. **Load it onto the board.** Send the bitstream to the FPGA (often over a USB/JTAG cable) and the chip becomes your circuit.

Along the way you read two simple report cards. **Utilization** says how much of the chip you used (how many LUTs, flip-flops, RAM, DSP). **Timing** says whether the circuit is fast enough; its key number is **slack** (covered next).

**Recap:** HDL -> simulate -> synthesize -> place and route -> bitstream -> load. Check utilization and timing as you go.

items.prompt: What is the correct order of the FPGA implementation flow?
choices: Write HDL -> simulate -> synthesize -> place and route -> generate bitstream -> load on board
Generate bitstream -> simulate -> write HDL -> load on board -> synthesize
Load on board -> place and route -> write HDL -> simulate
Synthesize -> write HDL -> load on board -> simulate -> bitstream
items.explanation: You describe the circuit (HDL), check it in a simulator, translate it to chip blocks (synthesis), place and wire those blocks (place and route), pack the result into a bitstream, and load it. Each step depends on the one before, so the order is fixed.

items.prompt: What does synthesis do?
choices: Translates your HDL into the FPGA's actual building blocks (LUTs, flip-flops, RAM, DSP), producing a netlist
Loads the finished bitstream onto the board
Feeds test inputs into the design and checks the outputs
Decides the exact physical location of every block on the chip
items.explanation: Synthesis maps your text description onto the fabric's real resources and outputs a netlist (a list of blocks and connections). Testing inputs is simulation, choosing physical spots is place and route, and loading is the final step.

items.prompt: What is a testbench used for in simulation?
choices: It feeds inputs into your design and checks that the outputs are correct, before you use real hardware
It is the physical board you plug the FPGA into
It is the final binary configuration file
It assigns each block to a physical location on the chip
items.explanation: A testbench is test code that drives your design with inputs and verifies the outputs in a simulator, so you can find logic bugs quickly. It is not hardware, not the bitstream, and not the placement step.

items.prompt: Why simulate before building the design onto the chip?
choices: Finding and fixing a bug in simulation is far faster than rebuilding and testing on the board
Simulation is the only way to ever run the design
The board cannot run HDL designs at all
Simulation produces the bitstream directly
items.explanation: A simulator lets you see every signal and re-run in seconds, while a full build-and-test cycle on the board can take minutes to hours and shows much less detail. So you catch most bugs in simulation first. Simulation does not produce a bitstream or replace running on real hardware.

items.prompt: Which step is usually the slowest and hardest, and why?
choices: Place and route, because the tool must choose physical spots for every block and find legal wiring among millions of options
Writing the HDL, because typing is slow
Loading the bitstream, because the cable is slow
Simulation, because it always runs for hours
items.explanation: Place and route is a huge optimization problem: where to put each block and how to connect them while still being fast enough. That is why it dominates build time. Typing HDL, loading the bitstream, and a quick simulation are comparatively fast.

items.prompt: What does the bitstream-generation step produce?
choices: The binary configuration file that, when loaded, makes the FPGA become your circuit
The HDL source code for your design
A report of how much of the chip you used
The testbench used during simulation
items.explanation: Bitstream generation packs the placed-and-routed design into the binary the chip loads to configure itself. The HDL is what you wrote at the start, the utilization report is separate, and the testbench is for simulation.

items.prompt: What does the utilization report tell you?
choices: How much of the chip's resources (LUTs, flip-flops, RAM, DSP) your design uses
Whether every signal arrives in time for the clock
Which physical pin connects to the LED
How long the USB cable is
items.explanation: Utilization is a resource report: it shows how full the chip is. Whether signals arrive in time is the timing report (slack). Pin assignment is in the constraints, and cable length is irrelevant.

items.prompt: How does loading a bitstream actually configure the FPGA?
choices: It sets the contents of every LUT and the state of the programmable wiring so the generic fabric becomes the described circuit
It permanently etches new transistors onto the silicon
It installs an operating system that runs your design as software
It changes the board's power-supply voltage
items.explanation: The bitstream loads the LUT contents and routing switches into the chip, turning the generic fabric into your specific design. No silicon is etched (that is an ASIC), and the FPGA runs as hardware, not as software on an OS.

items.prompt: A beginner says "I'll skip simulation and just program the board." What is the main risk?
choices: Debugging on the board is much slower and shows far less detail, so simple logic bugs take much longer to find
The board will be physically damaged by any logic bug
The bitstream cannot be generated without simulating first
Synthesis will refuse to run
items.explanation: Skipping simulation is allowed, but on-board debugging is slow (each fix needs a full rebuild) and you can only see signals you specifically route out. So bugs that would take seconds to spot in simulation can take hours on hardware. The build steps themselves do not require simulation.

items.prompt: Read the flow diagram. After place and route succeeds, what is the next step before the chip can run your design?
choices: Generate the bitstream, then load it onto the board
Re-write the HDL from scratch
Run synthesis again from the beginning
Nothing — place and route already makes the chip run
items.explanation: Once blocks are placed and wired, the tool packs the result into a bitstream, which you then load onto the board to configure it. Place and route alone does not change the physical chip; only loading the bitstream does.

lessons.title: Reading the Results: Timing Slack and Resource Use
lessons.content:
After the tools build your design, how do you know it is good? You read two report cards: **timing** (is it fast enough?) and **utilization** (does it fit?).

**Timing and slack.** Inside the chip, data has to travel from one flip-flop to the next and settle *before* the next clock tick arrives. Think of catching a train: the data must reach the platform before the train leaves. **Slack** is the spare time you have. If data arrives with time to spare, slack is **positive** — good, it makes the train. If data arrives too late, slack is **negative** — a problem; the design may give wrong answers at that clock speed.

In numbers, $\\text{slack} = \\text{time available} - \\text{time the signal actually needs}$. Positive means margin; negative means a timing failure. If timing fails, you can run the clock slower (give more time), simplify the logic, or split a long path into smaller steps (called pipelining). One subtlety: a *hold* problem (data arriving too soon) cannot be fixed by slowing the clock — only a *setup* problem (data too late) is helped by a slower clock.

**Utilization.** This says how full the chip is: what fraction of the LUTs, flip-flops, block RAM, and DSP blocks your design uses. If you try to use more than the chip has, it simply will not fit. And even before you hit 100%, packing the chip very full (say above ~80-90%) makes the wiring crowded, so place and route slows down and timing gets harder. Good practice: leave some room.

The tools also need to know your intended clock speed (you state it in a small **constraints** file) and which design signals connect to which physical **pins** (LEDs, buttons, the clock input). A wrong pin assignment is the classic reason a design works in simulation but the LED never lights.

**Recap:** positive slack = fast enough; negative = too slow. Utilization = how full the chip is; leave headroom.

items.prompt: What does timing slack measure?
choices: The spare time between when a signal arrives and when it was needed
How many LUTs the design uses
How long the bitstream file is
How many pins the chip has
items.explanation: Slack is the margin in time: how much earlier (or later) the data arrives compared to the deadline set by the clock. It is a timing quantity, not a count of resources, file size, or pins.

items.prompt: Your timing report shows positive slack on every path. What does that mean?
choices: Every signal settles in time at the target clock speed — timing passes
The design is too slow and will give wrong answers
The chip is completely full
The bitstream is corrupted
items.explanation: Positive slack means data arrives with time to spare before each clock tick, so the design meets its timing at the requested speed. Negative slack would be the failure case. Slack says nothing directly about how full the chip is or whether a file is corrupt.

items.prompt: A path has time available 8.0 ns and the signal actually needs 9.3 ns. What is its slack, and is it good?
choices: -1.3 ns, a timing failure (too slow)
+1.3 ns, passing with margin
+17.3 ns, easily passing
0 ns, exactly on the edge and fine
items.explanation: Slack = available - needed = 8.0 - 9.3 = -1.3 ns. Negative slack means the data arrives later than the deadline, so the design is not guaranteed to work at that clock. You would slow the clock, simplify logic, or pipeline the path.

items.prompt: Timing is failing (negative slack) because a signal arrives too late. Which fix can help?
choices: Run the clock slower to give the signal more time
Make the clock faster so it finishes sooner
Add more LEDs to the board
Delete the constraints file entirely
items.explanation: Data arriving too late is a setup problem; giving it more time by slowing the clock directly helps (so does simplifying or pipelining the logic). A faster clock makes it worse. LEDs are unrelated, and deleting constraints just hides the problem rather than fixing it.

items.prompt: What does the utilization report tell you?
choices: What fraction of the chip's LUTs, flip-flops, RAM, and DSP your design uses
Whether each signal arrives before the clock tick
Which vendor made the chip
How many lines of HDL you wrote
items.explanation: Utilization reports how full the chip is across each resource type. Whether signals arrive in time is the timing report. The vendor and your HDL line count are not what utilization measures.

items.prompt: Why is packing a chip to about 90% utilization usually a bad idea?
choices: The wiring gets crowded, so place and route slows down and meeting timing becomes harder
It makes the chip use less power and run faster automatically
It shrinks the bitstream file to almost nothing
It guarantees all timing problems disappear
items.explanation: When the chip is nearly full, the router has few free wires left and must take long detours, which adds delay and makes timing closure fragile and slow. Leaving headroom helps. A full chip does not magically lower power or fix timing.

items.prompt: What does the constraints file tell the tools?
choices: Your intended clock speed and which design signals connect to which physical pins
The full truth table of every LUT
The contents of every block RAM at power-up
How to write your HDL
items.explanation: The constraints file states the timing intent (clock period) and maps top-level signals to physical pins and I/O standards. Without a clock constraint, the timing report is meaningless. LUT contents come from synthesis, not constraints.

items.prompt: Your design is perfect in simulation, but on the board the LED never lights. What is the most likely cause?
choices: A wrong or missing pin assignment, so the design's output is not connected to the LED's pin
The simulator computed the logic incorrectly
Block RAM is full
The clock constraint period is too long
items.explanation: Simulation does not know about physical pins; only the constraints file maps signals to pins. A wrong or missing pin assignment is the classic reason hardware behaves differently from a correct simulation. A full RAM or a long clock period would not specifically explain a dead LED.

items.prompt: Which best describes the difference between the timing report and the utilization report?
choices: Timing says whether the design is fast enough; utilization says whether it fits on the chip
Timing counts pins; utilization counts clock edges
They are two names for the same report
Timing measures power; utilization measures temperature
items.explanation: These are the two main report cards: timing (does every path meet the clock?) and utilization (how much of the chip's resources are used?). Together they tell you if the design both runs at speed and fits. They are not the same report, and neither measures power or temperature directly.

items.prompt: Splitting one long logic path into several shorter stages so each fits within a clock tick is called:
choices: Pipelining
Synthesis
Routing congestion
Configuration
items.explanation: Pipelining breaks a long path into shorter stages (with flip-flops between them) so each stage settles within one clock period, helping meet timing. Synthesis is HDL-to-blocks translation, routing congestion is crowded wiring, and configuration is loading the bitstream.

lessons.title: FPGA vs CPU vs GPU vs ASIC, and Why Parallelism Wins
lessons.content:
An FPGA is not always the best chip for a job. Engineers pick among four kinds of chip, each with a sweet spot.

- **CPU / MCU** (the brain in most computers and small devices): cheap, low power, and easy to program in C, but it runs steps roughly one at a time. Great for control and modest workloads; poor at huge non-stop number-crunching.
- **GPU** (the graphics-and-AI chip): has thousands of small cores and enormous throughput on big batches of math, but it uses a lot of power and its response time is high and uneven. Great for offline batch work; awkward when you must react instantly and predictably.
- **FPGA**: reconfigurable parallel hardware. Lower power than a GPU, with **low and predictable** response time, and you can re-program it in the field. No huge upfront factory cost. Wins for low-to-medium volumes, changing requirements, and real-time streaming.
- **ASIC** (a chip custom-built for one job at the factory): the best speed, power, and per-unit cost — but it costs a fortune to design and *cannot be changed* once made. Only worth it at very high volume.

A simple rule: need flexibility and fast iteration? FPGA. Just need easy control and low cost? CPU/MCU. Massive offline math? GPU. Millions of identical units where every cent and milliwatt counts? ASIC.

**Why FPGAs shine at high-speed signal processing.** Many real-time tasks (like filtering a fast stream of measurements) repeat the same multiply-and-add over and over. A CPU does those one after another. An FPGA can lay down, say, 128 multipliers that *all* compute at the same time, every clock tick, keeping up with the incoming data with steady, predictable timing. That "many operations at once, on time, every time" property is exactly why FPGAs are favored for fast parallel signal processing.

**Vendor tools (brief).** AMD/Xilinx uses **Vivado**; Intel/Altera uses **Quartus**; smaller Lattice chips have a free **open-source** toolchain (Yosys + nextpnr). All take HDL through the same flow you learned.

**Recap:** match the chip to the job — flexibility/parallelism (FPGA), ease/cost (CPU), batch throughput (GPU), volume (ASIC).

items.prompt: When does an FPGA beat a CPU/MCU?
choices: When you need lots of operations at once with steady, predictable timing on a fast data stream
When you want the cheapest, simplest chip for light control tasks
When you only ever run one instruction at a time
When you want to write everything in C with no hardware knowledge
items.explanation: An FPGA wins when heavy parallel work must keep up with a fast stream at predictable timing, because it builds many operators that run at once. For cheap, simple, easy-to-program control, the CPU/MCU is the better pick.

items.prompt: What is the key downside of an ASIC compared with an FPGA?
choices: It costs a fortune to design and cannot be changed once manufactured
It is always slower and uses more power than an FPGA
It can never be made in large quantities
It must be reprogrammed every time it powers on
items.explanation: An ASIC offers the best speed, power, and per-unit cost, but only after a very expensive design effort, and it is fixed forever once made. An FPGA trades some efficiency for being reprogrammable with near-zero upfront cost. ASICs are actually best at very high volume.

items.prompt: A GPU has huge raw throughput, so why might an FPGA still be chosen for real-time streaming?
choices: The FPGA gives low and predictable response time, while a GPU's latency is high and uneven
The FPGA always has more cores than a GPU
A GPU cannot do math at all
The FPGA runs at a far higher clock speed than any GPU
items.explanation: GPUs excel at big batches but have high, variable latency (and high power), which is awkward when you must react instantly and on time. An FPGA processes each piece of data with steady, predictable timing, which fits real-time streaming. It is not about having more cores or a higher clock.

items.prompt: A startup needs about 2,000 units of a product whose features are still changing, and it must handle a fast data stream in real time. Which chip fits best?
choices: FPGA — parallel, predictable timing, reprogrammable, and low upfront cost
ASIC — lowest per-unit cost
CPU/MCU — cheapest and simplest
GPU — most raw throughput
items.explanation: Low-to-medium volume plus changing features rules out the ASIC's huge fixed design cost and frozen function. Real-time fast streaming is hard for a sequential CPU and for a GPU's uneven latency. The FPGA's reprogrammable parallel hardware fits all three needs.

items.prompt: A company will ship 50 million identical units of a fixed-function chip where cost and power per unit matter most. Best choice?
choices: ASIC — best per-unit cost and power once the high design cost is spread over millions of units
FPGA — because it can be reprogrammed later
GPU — because of raw throughput
CPU/MCU — because it is easy to program
items.explanation: At very high volume the ASIC's large one-time design cost is divided over millions of units, and it gives the best per-unit cost and power for a fixed function. Reprogrammability (FPGA) is not needed here, and a GPU or CPU would not match an ASIC's efficiency at this scale.

items.prompt: Why can a 128-tap filter (128 multiply-and-adds per sample) run comfortably on an FPGA but stress a single CPU core?
choices: The FPGA can place 128 multipliers that all compute every clock tick, while the CPU does the 128 operations one after another
The FPGA's clock is 128 times faster than the CPU's
Filters cannot run on a CPU at all
The CPU has no way to multiply numbers
items.explanation: An FPGA spends area to build many operators that work in parallel each clock, producing a result per tick. A CPU core must perform the operations in sequence, so throughput drops as the count grows. It is parallel hardware, not a faster clock, that wins.

items.prompt: Which property makes FPGAs especially good for high-speed parallel signal processing?
choices: They lay down many multiply-and-add units that all run at once, keeping up with a fast stream with predictable timing
They store the whole data stream in a single flip-flop
They run C code faster than any CPU
They have an unlimited number of pins
items.explanation: Signal processing repeats multiply-and-add operations; an FPGA builds many of them to run concurrently every clock, matching the data rate with steady latency. A single flip-flop holds only one bit, FPGAs do not run C as software, and pin count is not the point.

items.prompt: Which statement about choosing a chip is correct?
choices: CPU/MCU for easy low-cost control; GPU for big offline batch math; FPGA for flexible real-time parallelism; ASIC for very high volume
ASIC for prototypes that change often; CPU for the fastest parallel streaming
GPU for the lowest-power tiny control task; FPGA only for storing files
All four chips are interchangeable with identical strengths
items.explanation: Each chip has a sweet spot: CPU/MCU (cheap, easy control), GPU (batch throughput), FPGA (flexible, predictable parallelism), ASIC (high volume). ASICs are wrong for changing prototypes, GPUs are not low-power control chips, and the four are clearly not interchangeable.

items.prompt: Which pair correctly matches an FPGA vendor to its main design tool?
choices: AMD/Xilinx -> Vivado; Intel/Altera -> Quartus
AMD/Xilinx -> Quartus; Intel/Altera -> Vivado
Both AMD/Xilinx and Intel/Altera -> Photoshop
Neither vendor provides any design tool
items.explanation: AMD/Xilinx parts use Vivado and Intel/Altera parts use Quartus; smaller Lattice parts have an open-source flow (Yosys + nextpnr). They all run HDL through the same basic flow. Photoshop is image-editing software, unrelated to FPGAs.

items.prompt: What is one advantage of the open-source FPGA toolchain (Yosys + nextpnr)?
choices: It is free and scriptable, with no license wall, for supported Lattice parts
It only works on the most expensive chips
It replaces the need to write any HDL
It removes the place-and-route step entirely
items.explanation: Open-source tools like Yosys and nextpnr are free and easy to automate, with no license restrictions, for supported (often smaller Lattice) parts. They still use HDL and still perform place and route — they just do it with an open, scriptable flow.
